Document [3] (Title: Breast Cancer -- Medical Oncology -- Targeted Therapy) Anti-HER2 therapy is indicated in 17% of breast cancers that overproduce the growth-promoting protein HER2/neu. Trastuzumab, the first approved drug, is a monoclonal antibody directly targeting the HER2 protein. It reduces the risk of recurrence and death by 52% and 33%, respectively, if combined with chemotherapy in HER2-positive early breast cancer if compared to chemotherapy alone. [70] [71] More recent data advocates for dual HER2 blockade with trastuzumab and pertuzumab, which improves response rates.

[8] [9] [10] [11] According to the American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO), HER2-targeted therapy is recommended for patients with HER2-positive advanced breast cancer, except for patients with congestive heart failure or a significantly reduced left ventricular ejection fraction, which should be evaluated individually. As a first-line treatment, ASCO recommends trastuzumab, pertuzumab, and taxanes. Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1082) Pharmacologic substances comprise a diverse group of therapeutic agents that include ethinylestradiol, barbituráty (barbiturates), brentuximab vedotin, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, doxorubicin hydrochloride, ifosfamid, mechlorethamin, pembrolizumab, sargramostim, streptozocin, vinblastin, and vincristine sulfate. Barbituráty are classified as a subclass of pharmacologic substances, reflecting their role as central‑nervous‑system depressants. Ethinylestradiol is an estrogenic compound commonly used in hormonal contraception and hormone replacement therapy. Brentuximab vedotin is an antibody‑drug conjugate employed in the treatment of CD30‑positive lymphomas. Cyclophosphamide anhydrous and ifosfamid are alkylating agents that function as cytotoxic chemotherapy drugs for a variety of malignancies. Doxorubicin hydrochloride is an anthracycline antibiotic used widely in cancer chemotherapy for its DNA‑intercalating and free‑radical generating properties. Mechlorethamin (mechlorethamine) is a nitrogen‑mustard alkylating agent historically used in lymphoma therapy. Pembrolizumab is a programmed death‑1 (PD‑1) immune checkpoint inhibitor that enhances antitumor immune responses. Sargramostim is a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor (GM‑CSF) that stimulates white‑blood‑cell production, often used to mitigate chemotherapy‑induced neutropenia. Streptozocin is an alkylating agent with particular activity against pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. Vinblastin and vincristine sulfate are vinca‑alkaloid chemotherapeutics that disrupt microtubule formation, thereby inhibiting cell division in a range of cancers. Recognizing these agents as pharmacologic substances and understanding their distinct mechanisms of action supports clinicians in selecting appropriate treatments, anticipating therapeutic effects, and managing potential adverse events in patient care.
